217907309.review: Great timeloop game with lots of aha-moments that felt VERY satisfying.

217826731.review:
Excellent time loop game with some super clever ideas. It has a good mix of puzzles within each of its discrete zones/areas that lead to items/knowledge/shortcuts that expedite subsequent loops or help solve bigger, more overreaching puzzles. (definitely recommend a notepad for this one, and writing down anything that seems like it might be key information - particularly helpful when going for the best ending). 

The 10 move loop is short enough that I felt free to experiment and never felt like I was losing progress. If you do happen to accidentally mess up (the main culprit being forgetting to lock down one of your items to carry it between loops), it's not a big deal to get it back since the main currency in Chronoquartz is knowledge. The short loop also led to some nice "aha!" moments where I thought there was no way I could accomplish what I needed in so few moves, but then felt quite clever after executing a plan and making it in the nick of time.

Really satisfying game that kept me quite engaged from start to finish, I chewed through it in basically 2 long sittings. Big thumbs up from me, highly recommend!

217692107.review:
🔸 EXPECTATIONS: I had no expectations.
🔸 REALITY: a cross between Minit and SNES Zelda, with no combat.

🔷 Chronoquartz is a top-down puzzle adventure game with no combat where the protagonist can only reach up to 10 screens (think of screens as in old Zelda games). When the 10th is reached, the world rewinds/resets, but as a player, the knowledge learned is kept.

🔹 Of course, as the game progresses the player overcomes some restrictions, but new ones are added, that being one of the greatest aspects of the game: it's rather short, but it's very varied, making the overall very interesting and fun.

🔹 It has 5 distinctive areas, each taking about 1h to complete, and 2 endings. These areas share the '10 screens' rule but are played a bit differently, specially when it comes to solving its puzzles, adding new rules/restrictions and quirky tools to overcome them.

🔹 The 'puzzle' part of the game comes in the shape of riddles, the gathering (and piecing together) of clues and navigating the environments, as some backtracking within each area is required. I'd say it's difficulty is medium, but it includes a hints system in case of need.

🔹 Its art and music are nice, although some visual elements appearing at completely different resolutions (on purpose, I believe) had me confused at times.

💠 Chronoquartz is a game very easy to recommend: nice art, good story, great gameplay, perfect length... unless you absolutely hate having to think on your adventure game, you won't go wrong with it.

POSITIVES:
🙂+
😄+ varied gameplay
😍+
NEGATIVES:
🙁-
😠-
😡-

217161036.review:
... So I just finished this one!
I think I did... or will I? 
Time travel is confusing! Luckily the game itself isn't that confusing as long as you keep your wits sharp. I am a sucker for these kind of games where you can redo puzzles over and over again and essentially skip gathering the hints once you discover the solution, find a shortcut or bypass the area altogether. In Chronoquartz you have basically 10 turns (aka screen transitions) to finish the game and prevent evil from destroying the world. Sounds daunting at first but since you can loop to your hearts content you have all the time in the world! See what I did there? 

Anyway this game is first and foremost a puzzle game with no combat whatsoever so the only skill that's tested here is your brainpower. Even more important than solving the actual puzzles is keeping track on their solutions. A notepad while playing is highly recommended so you don't have to figure out the way of solving it yet again. Beside the out game inventory your inventory in game is important as well since you can carry a few chosen items with you through the loops if you manage to find inventory locks. Otherwise you can kiss them goodbye and have to reacquire them again. This is of course not a big deal and might set you back 2-3 loops so there is no real reason to stress out if you made a mistake. Everything can be corrected it just takes a little time (pun intended!).

The puzzles themselves always felt solvable with no real way to get absolutely stuck. At least in my case I was stumped doing certain puzzles for maybe 10 loops at the most but after reviewing my notes and carefully thinking about the issue it clicked in the end. And let me tell you once that happens it's a really nice feeling. Even better when you go after the true ending since that almost feels like some meta level stuff and I enjoyed that very much. 

I vaguely heard about this game during a conversation, had to check it out and must admit that I was very much entertained. It's a shorter game of course but worth your time for 1-2 evenings or a longer afternoon. It does what it wants to do very well and does not overstay it's welcome. 7.5/10 and the price of roughly 10 bucks is more than fair. No need to wait for a discount. If you are a friend of some quick puzzles go ahead and grab it! ...

...So I just finished this one!

216713075.review:
It's a good knowledge-gated game. Short but sweet. Actually I think the game's limited scope is to it's benefit. It makes it easier to consider all the info you have available to solve puzzles. 

That said, I think the game could benefit from two features/improvements: 

1. Allow players to pin info/screenshots on the map. The map is generally good at showing you the detail in each zone to help you keep track of stuff, but it doesn't show certain things like messages which you had to interact with a person or object to read. This feels a bit arbitrary. My solution to this was to just take screenshots and put them in a document anyway, so making it a built in feature would be nice.

2. On repeat playthroughs of the game, it would be nice to have the option to play with the full map revealed. This is info we've already acquired and could have screenshotted with some foresight, but once I started a new save and overrode my past one, I lost that chance. 

With the exception of one or two parts, the puzzles are generally solved once you understand what is going on. There isn't a lot of trial and error necessary to complete them. 

Spoiling as little as possible, the ending was interesting but somewhat disappointing. I think it made good use of the overall mechanic/structure of the game, however, the actual solution turned out to be fairly trivial. 

Overall, solid recommend if you like these kinds of games.

Excellent little time loop puzzle game. Sort of like Minit, but here you have 10 turns before the timeline resets, instead of 60 seconds. Moving from one screen or doing certain actions skips a turn forward. Mechanically functions almost exactly like Minit, with shortcuts, ways to get around the turn limit, new spawn points and so on. 216522689.review:
An excellent puzzle game, but you should know some things going in.

   This is not a quick casual playthrough.  There is an excellent hint system in game, but I think the game was designed by a person who thought about how to craft puzzles for a long time, and you're going to need to think about some of them for a while.  My advice is play through a puzzle section and pay very close attention to details.  

   I think the developer was quite inventive and stretched some mechanics beyond the basics.  A little experimentation and patience is needed for the puzzles, and a pen and paper are good to write a few things down.  Still, its fresh and largely avoids tedium.  Also in general the scope of the puzzles always felt fair without moon logic.  

   So if you're a casual puzzle fan, give it a try.  You might really like it.  Moderate puzzlers and those who just like a fresh take should give this one a go.

216434322.review:
[i]Chronoquartz[/i] is a rare game that [b][i]really[/i] understands the time loop fantasy[/b]. It's also one of those perfect single-weekend games: it has a central idea it executes perfectly in a package that doesn't overstay its welcome.

[h1]Great execution (with some minor issues)[/h1]

[h2]The bad things[/h2]

Well, now it's more of a list of minor annoyances.

[olist]
[*]Controls [b]used to be[/b] a much bigger issue, but this has been ammeliorated. While there still are no keybindings and the Controls info card doesn't show all the keys, the game is no longer WSAD-only: you can move with arrow keys too. You can probably play it now even with only the right hand, since mouse is only used in menus, there are options to turn Space/Shift holds into toggles, and the game doesn't require simultaneous movement and pressing Space/Shift.
[*]The forest tileset for the crossroads uses a different pixel size than the rest of the game, which feels [b]very garish[/b].
[/olist]

And that's it.

[h2]The great things[/h2]

[i]Chronoquartz[/i] is [b]a great puzzle game[/b] that has some real stumpers and some red herrings (all in a good fun), and I beat it without hints or looking up walkthroughs. (But you [i]really[/i] need to [b]take notes[/b].)

It's also [b]a great time loop game[/b] since it understands the core appeal of time loops: you bumble your way through, solving minor problems, experimenting, gathering knowledge both knowingly and unwittingly, and then it [b]all comes together[/b] when you finally know enough to execute [i]that one final perfect loop[/i]. Perfect execution, no notes.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]In conclusion,[/h2]

[i]Chronoquartz[/i] is a better time loop game than the ones I've played ([i]The Sexy Brutale[/i], [i]Omensight[/i]) and possibly some AAA ones too (reportedly [i]Deathloop[/i] was extremely handholdy about its final loop?).

216057907.review:
Chronoquartz is an interesting miniature. A member of a new genre of games that combines in-game character progression with out-of-game player progression, it is perhaps most reminiscent of Vision Soft Reset. That said, it is its own experience.

In terms of graphics and audio, Chronoquartz is "retro." The spritework and music are both fine, though sometimes the lighting effects are unpleasant. The controls also qualify as merely "fine," with some touchiness around dragging objects. Luckily, with thoughtful play, mechanical skill is never necessary. This is not a AAA game that's going to wow you with its immersion.

The heart of the game is its puzzling, which is well-executed. As a miniature game (longer than a demo, but about 5 hours to 100%) it never scales up to anything diabolically difficult, but what's there is mostly satisfying. There is one sub-area (the desert) with some more obtuse puzzles, but these can be looked up if you're stumped without really detracting from the rest of the game. The game's final moments, when it asks you to really take advantage of all that you've learned exploring its mechanics, are well-executed and satisfying.

Overall, Chronoquartz is a game which will likely be looked back on as "an early example of..." once the genre has a proper name and more examples. Right now, however, it is one of the best executed of a small subset, and as such highly recommended.

215997928.review:
A fun little time loop based puzzle game. Give it a go over the weekend if you like games like the Sexy Brutale you won't regret it

Even if you don't you can always turn back time till you do!

215977184.review: Not a very long game, but executes what it does pretty well. I had fun with the puzzles, the difficulty seemed appropriate for what it is, and the time loop/resets never really got frustrating.

215854589.review: It's a fun game. It took me 413 loops my first playthrough.

215781869.review:
This is a really cool game. It is a little short and I wouldn't mind it being a little longer (though not necessary). I will surely recommend it to anyone who can enjoy a puzzle game.

Puzzles were fun and the general concept of juggling items in a time loop while keeping track of new information was really amazing. It is surprising how inventory limit combined with time constraint turned otherwise boring backtracking into the routing puzzle. It wasn't crazy or anything but it was in fact very interesting to do the routing and I think there is an opportunity for another game which would further explore the idea of "backtracking is fun if routing is an engaging challenge in itself".

Coming back to the game it worth noting that other stuff is a solid work as well: music is decent, art style is contained and looks good -- there are no annoying inconsistencies which many other indie happen to have. Character movement is smooth and no gameplay element seem to get in the way.

The things I want to critique are below. They are very much spoilers to the game mechanics and puzzles so if you are interested in the game, consider ignoring them. There is nothing even remotely deal breaking -- simply the feedback.

1. When using slow-motion, music slows down and it seems that sound quality of the slowed music drops sharply. With a very expensive (and sensitive) sound system using this item made me a little nauseous. Not sure if this is in fact sound quality or simply games ost is not mixing well with the slowdown but it felt bad to the point of muting game for this segments
2. Desert cave puzzle is way too open in my opinion. I assume using hints is not the intended experience. In this case I don't think there is a short way to rule out that the code is "do 4 directional actions with some plates and enter the cave -- if actions were correct you would be transported elsewhere". I think the main issue is that there is no indication that cave entering is the method of submitting one piece of the code and not the whole code. I think if there was some lizard implying that or you had to use the cave for two reasons with the first one solution  being basically handed to you or somehow making it obvious that arrow means which cave entrance to use e.g. having code with 4 empty plates and simply directions (but idk if it would help me), it would make the puzzle okay but possibly too obvious (I guess you still had to figure out what red blob means but there are not too many options and knowledge that correct entry gives a beep after entering the cave). I had a bad time with it but I don't have any other suggestion aside from those.
3. Language puzzle is probably also kinda polarizing -- if you guess it it feels like a cool idea but if you don't then tough luck. I guess it doesn't lock out the main progression so it is not a big deal.
4. The chronopass puzzle is also a bit weird. I guess it is not a big leap to read "code changes each loop" and then try to look at the code values and guess the sequence, but this is something I would expect in a job interview where the assumption is that you can come up with the solution based on problem formulation and not find it elsewhere. A big portion of the game is exactly the latter -- finding the solution somewhere else or finding a lot of information so that piecing together solution is a very tiny leap of logic. I wonder, how many people's instinct was that they gotta go find logic behind the chronopass code somewhere else (maybe behind the language cypher) or that they have missed another bit of the game and have to come back later (this actually happens with the final shortcut from the graveyard as you find it early but get to enter properly only post game). Having some of the map segments empty doesn't help this either. I think this puzzle requires too much of a leap in reasoning compared to the rest of the game. It could've been fine if there was more puzzles like this but this spike was not the best moment of the game.
5. Rainbow key assembly feels a little misleading at the moment -- you get the map icon of keys triangle in the house and it is drawn on the floor. I first assumed that  you have to bring 3 keys there but after trying to route it I realized that it seems impossible (or I didn't see something) to bring 3 keys there with 2 locks (you are forced to lock green and red key since you don't have a way back after getting them and you don't have enough turns to get the blue key from the prison checkpoint to the house) so I tried to do a leap of faith to see if they would just combine upon picking the 3rd key. I'd probably just erase the floor painting or at least removed it from the map -- it also looks outlined like an item (but maybe dementia moment again) which makes it look like a place of importance

Even though I wrote a lot more words about the negatives, I still enjoyed the game very much and I'm looking forward to playing more games from Infinite Stairs

215174407.review:
Chronoquartz, developed and published by Infinite Stairs, is a thoughtfully designed puzzle-adventure that builds its entire identity around constraint, planning, and the quiet satisfaction of intellectual mastery. Rather than overwhelming players with sprawling worlds or complex controls, Chronoquartz takes a more deliberate approach, centering its experience on a deceptively simple rule: each time loop grants a strictly limited number of actions. From that single limitation, the game constructs a layered, methodical journey that rewards patience, memory, and foresight above all else.

The core mechanic revolves around short, repeating time loops in which every movement and interaction consumes a turn. Walking across a room, opening a door, picking up an item, or activating a switch all count toward a fixed action budget before time resets. This structure immediately reframes exploration as a strategic exercise rather than a casual activity. Players are encouraged to think like planners, mapping out routes mentally and optimizing every step. Even the act of walking down a hallway carries weight, transforming what would normally be filler movement into a meaningful decision.

What makes the loop system compelling is that while the world resets, knowledge does not. Information learned in previous loops—such as door codes, hidden passages, enemy behavior, or puzzle solutions—persists in the player’s mind and becomes the primary form of progression. Over time, routes become more efficient, detours are eliminated, and seemingly impossible objectives begin to feel achievable. The game subtly teaches players that failure is not a setback but a data-gathering phase, reframing repetition as learning rather than punishment.

Puzzle design is where Chronoquartz truly shines. Early challenges introduce the fundamentals gently, teaching players how to conserve actions and prioritize goals. As the game progresses, puzzles become more intricate, often spanning multiple loops and requiring careful sequencing of actions. Some objectives demand that you set up conditions in one loop so they can be exploited in the next, while others rely on environmental observation and memory. The satisfaction of executing a perfect loop—where every action aligns and a long-standing obstacle is finally overcome—is one of the game’s most rewarding moments.

Exploration is tightly interwoven with these puzzles. The game’s world, centered around a mysterious castle and its surrounding areas, is compact but dense with purpose. Each room feels intentionally designed, with layouts that invite optimization and shortcuts that gradually reveal themselves as understanding deepens. Rather than expanding horizontally, the world opens up vertically in complexity, encouraging players to revisit familiar spaces with new insight rather than constantly introducing new locations.

Narrative elements are understated but effective. Chronoquartz does not rely on lengthy dialogue or overt exposition. Instead, story details are uncovered gradually through environmental cues, brief character encounters, and the implications of the time loop itself. The sense of being trapped in a repeating cycle, trying to avert an unseen catastrophe or uncover the truth behind the Chronoquartz artifact, adds emotional weight to the mechanical challenges without overshadowing them. The mystery unfolds quietly, rewarding curiosity rather than demanding attention.

Visually, the game adopts a clean, retro-inspired pixel art style that prioritizes clarity and readability. The restrained aesthetic works in the game’s favor, ensuring that puzzle elements, pathways, and interactable objects are easy to identify at a glance. Animations are subtle, and the color palette is used thoughtfully to distinguish important details without visual clutter. Complementing this is a minimalist sound design that reinforces the contemplative mood, allowing moments of silence to heighten tension and focus.

Pacing is one of Chronoquartz’s greatest strengths. The short length of each loop encourages experimentation without overwhelming commitment, making it easy to jump in for brief sessions or settle in for longer problem-solving stretches. Difficulty increases steadily, but rarely feels unfair; when mistakes happen, they are usually traceable to inefficiency or oversight rather than opaque logic. That said, the game does demand a particular mindset—players unwilling to plan ahead or tolerate repetition may find the early stages challenging until the rhythm of learning and optimization becomes clear.

There are moments where the strict action limit can feel unforgiving, especially before players fully grasp how much efficiency matters. A single unnecessary step can invalidate an otherwise solid plan, forcing another loop. However, this rigidity is also central to the game’s identity, and once internalized, it becomes a source of tension rather than frustration. Chronoquartz trusts players to rise to its challenge rather than softening its rules.

Ultimately, Chronoquartz is a game for players who enjoy thinking deeply about systems and deriving satisfaction from elegant solutions. It transforms limitation into creativity, repetition into progress, and simplicity into depth. By blending time-loop mechanics with deliberate puzzle design and atmospheric exploration, it delivers an experience that feels quietly confident and intellectually rewarding. For fans of logic-driven adventures and games that respect the player’s intelligence, Chronoquartz stands as a distinctive and memorable entry in the puzzle genre.

Rating: 9/10

A really, really good time-loop puzzle game. Long enough to be satisfying but short enough that it never needs to start reusing mechanics beyond the point where they're interesting. The actual puzzles are mostly excellent, with only one exception -- the cave -- which, ironically, is the only time the clues consist of actual, step-by-step directions.

The final "metapuzzle" of reaching the True Ending is also very, very clever, and a really good capstone to the game. If you like time loop games where (almost) the only thing you can carry from loop to loop is your knowledge, you will 100% enjoy Chronoquartz.

214054090.review:
This is a really cool little knowledge-gated game, which is a style of puzzle solving that I really love and find impressive every time.

There are a lot of really neat ideas here, but just a little bit too much friction or annoyance in some places. There's a pretty solid help/hint system built into the game though that got me through a couple instances of that. The way you get the "good ending" is also a really smart idea, but with enough of that friction that it didn't completely hit for me.

Love seeing games like this though, I need to seek out more.

The game definitely requires you to pay attention so that you learn all the lessons you need to make more progress in subsequent loops. When you first launch it, you will smile. Couple of hours later you will realize you played the whole thing in one sitting and didn't stop smiling for the entire time. Most of the game is mechanically very simple, but every now and then you will get the massive "wait, thats how it works?!" or "I could do this the whole time?!" moment, which feels very rewarding.

209887590.review: Really Neat and Fun Puzzler! If stuck in the first area, [spoiler] remember the sword! [/spoiler]

209801162.review:
That game is a little gem !
The main gimmick is very interesting and very well executed with quite some variations here and there to keep it fresh. And it also integrates with the story, though it's quite a shallow story.
Anyway, you're not here for the story but the puzzles, and on this point it delivers. Puzzles are often not too easy, some are quite head scratching, but nothing too extreme. And many use the main gimmick in a unique way.
And it's a game that really makes you grow : if you know what to do, you can finish it in mere minutes, Outer Wilds style. If you don't ... Well, you have hours to learn by taking many roundabouts ! And there's a hint system that looks pretty well done, if you don't do thinks the wrong way (if you play the demo before, don't "skip" demo content in the main game by using your knowledge like I did or the hint system will get lost. It's pretty quick to redo things anyway with the acquired knowledge)

tl;dr :
++ Amazing gimmick
++ Well thought puzzles that always feel fresh
+ The feeling of progress through the game despite no "metaprogression"
+ You have to find what to do and how to progress by yourself : even if there are a few hints here and there, you're not handheld
+ Dev responded quickly to my plea of help when the hint system got lost
+ The game remembers the passwords, no need to type them again !

- Even if it doesn't feel like it too much, your progression is linear
- "Losing" some hard earned items by negligence (but getting them back was pretty quick, just a bit of a bore)

209768151.review:
A timeloop room loop loop loop game. 

Your just a little blue guy trying to save the kingdom from Lizard man from Soul Caliber. I enjoyed the premise but the time loop function of only being able to progress 10 rooms before being reset became a bit frustrating. 

If you like puzzles and time loops this game is for you.

209653021.review: Solid little puzzle game, had some frustrating bits but was overall satisfying at the end and i came away enjoying my time, bit of a tip: use the steam notes feature or a notepad to write down some stuff

209501566.review:
Good game overall.

209480263.review:
This was so much fun! Chronoquartz is a puzzle adventure / Zelda-like / metroidbrainia game where you have to save time itself from demons in a quasi-medieval fantasy world. You are in a time loop and you have a limited amount of times you can move from a screen to another.

I was honestly a bit skeptical at first, because the art looked like it came from a variety of asset packs that don't necessarily match (the pixel sizes are different on different objects). But I've seen buzz around this game in my social media bubbles and decided to give it a try - thank you social media bubbles, you were right.

The enjoyment of this type of game really comes down to the quality of puzzles / thinky elements, and here the puzzles were great, just a lot of fun. I laughed out loud at multiple points when I figured something out, in an "I canNOT believe it!" sense. The storyline is a basic fight-evil fantasy story, but there are some fun twists involving time manipulation. It is all-ages appropriate - if you want to play something with your kid/s that is also fun for adults, this is a great choice. The game was clearly inspired by Outer Wilds and there are some entertaining Outer Wilds references sprinkled throughout (I don't think they spoil much about Outer Wilds if that's your worry), but it's also very much its own thing and has a very different approach to puzzles.

There is very little combat, this is not a Tunic-like. Technically one could say there is no combat. (Spoilers with details: [spoiler] Enemies can attack you, but you can't fight back, you have to run away. You get a sword, but you can't use it beyond showing it to people.[/spoiler] )

I played the game on a Deck docked to the TV with a PS4 controller, and I had no issues, it worked great.

Without spoilers, I recommend trying for the second ending too - I was a bit worried because I saw a review that said it looked too complicated, but I think it's absolutely doable and you get a lot of satisfaction once you get it right. It only added about half an hour to my playtime. I feel like a lot of people say the game takes 3-5 hours total, but I honestly took 9, maybe because I like to explore and try odd things? I also got stuck here and there. (I should've probably used the help system instead of trying to find something in a youtube walkthrough after giving up, because I tried the in-game hint system after completion and it was really quite good.) I found one bug that forced me to restart a loop, but could not consistently reproduce it. I got all achievements except one that seemed a bit annoying.

I would love to see more games from the developer, because this is a wonderful start. Someone with this quality of ideas should have a bigger budget to execute them. (Yes, the art does come from asset packs, they are all listed in the ending credits.)

For the "is it a metroidbrainia" crowd, yes, it is. There are a lot of knowledge unlocks, vaguely spoilery details: 
[spoiler]there are both simple 'find out the code' or 'here is a secret passage' kinds of unlocks, and also more structural ones, like 'these objects can trigger their function in different spots and you can solve multiple puzzles knowing this, including ones you've already solved in a different way'. There is no leveling up, but there are objects to pick up and use, though you can pick up everything from the beginning.[/spoiler] 
___
Source of the game: Bought with my own money

An absolutely fantastic puzzler, a perfect mixture of exploration, puzzles and mechanic learning.

208635262.review:
Great game. This is the type of game that aspiring game developers can play and believe making good games is possible. Perfect 2D puzzles and a very generous help system when the developer has out-thought you. Comparisons to Outer Wilds are apt mostly based on the metroid-brainia aspects and less on the time-loop mechanic.

Warning: if you play this after playing Blue Prince, the counter is NOT a "steps" mechanic. Very similar, but this is more a Majoras Mask time-based mechanic. If you think of it as purely time-based and not steps-based, you will have an easier time figuring out The Forge level than I did. :)

Looking forward to Infinite Stairs' next project!

208366310.review: Fun little time loop game. A couple annoying points where you have to redo a loop because you mess up right before achieving your goal, but there's some excellent puzzles in here.

208354714.review: An absolute blast of a gaming experience, worth every dollar. A puzzle-adventure game that really has the player using their noggin to figure out multi-room puzzles, when you can only be in so many rooms during each "loop". Kept fresh and new ideas on how to use the time loop mechanic with each area and never got stale. Puzzles were the exact right amount of challenging to be a hit of dopamine when solved, but never to hard to frustrate. 100% the game is about 5 hours, and I felt this was the perfect length for the lovely lil adventure.

208351317.review:
[h1] [b]Chronoquartz, a game to remember.[/b] [/h1]

[h2] Overview [/h2]

---{ Graphics }---
☐ You forget what reality is
☐ Beautiful
☑ Good
☐ Decent
☐ Bad
☐ Don‘t look too long at it
☐ MS-DOS

---{ Gameplay }---
☑ Very good
☐ Good
☐ It's just gameplay
☐ Mehh
☐ Watch paint dry instead
☐ Just don't

---{ Audio }---
☐ Eargasm
☑ Very good
☐ Good
☐ Not too bad
☐ Bad
☐ I'm now deaf

---{ Difficulty }---
☐ Just press 'W'
☐ Easy
☐ Easy to learn / Hard to master
☑ Significant brain usage
☐ Difficult
☐ Dark Souls

---{ Story }---
☐ No Story
☐ Some lore
☐ Average
☑ Good
☐ Lovely
☐ It'll replace your life

---{ Game Time }---
☐ Long enough for a cup of coffee
☑ Short
☐ Average
☐ Long
☐ To infinity and beyond

---{ Price }---
☐ It's free!
☑ Worth the price
☐ If it's on sale
☐ If u have some spare money left
☐ Not recommended
☐ You could also just burn your money

[h2] Quick summary. [/h2]

Chronoquartz surprised me in the best way possible. Being a fan of puzzle games, this showed some real promise - and delivered.
Visually, it's a very nice pixelated game. I really enjoy the simple yet neat style the developers went for.
The gameplay was instantly addictive; it's really smooth and requires real thought. I played it through with all achievements  in one sitting. There were moments when I spent 10 minutes thinking over some puzzle and then noticed that the solution was really simple.

Personally, I love the soundtrack; it is really awesome and totally fits with the vibe of a small pixel indie game.

It's only a short ride, but one to remember - definitely worth the price. I'd even pay 30 bucks for this game rather than many others out there. 

Verdict: 9/10 - A compact, clever gem that shines far brighter than its size suggests.

206655185.review:
Chronoquartz is a short but sweet, strategic puzzle game. Your goal is to stop the evil demon Morthas from conquering the world — but what's the catch? You have 10 turns. On turn 10, you reset and the loop begins anew — a Groundhog Day scenario if you will. While the puzzles aren't too complex, they will have you scratching your head trying to fit all the puzzle pieces together to achieve the desired result in the loop.

Each loop you will learn something new, and each time brings you a loop closer to stopping the demon and breaking the cycle.

205967141.review:
[h2]This timeloop metroidbrainia far exceeded my expectations[/h2]

[b]Difficulty:[/b] ★★★☆☆

What a game. Excellent and tight game design, with many fun and genuinely interesting puzzles based on a 10-step time loop mechanic and metroid elements. Each area offers something fresh, exciting, surprising and unexpected, with interesting items, interactions and mechanics. Taking notes of [i]certain[/i] things is highly recommended, and sometimes thinking outside of the box is mandatory. It is a true metriodbrainia, so make sure to jump in completely blind, spoiler-free. If you know the game, it is possible to beat in just a few minutes. 

It is not just a good game. It is an outstanding puzzle game, and for a very fair price. The devs should be proud :)

A nice puzzle game with very pleasant mechanics. It would take about 4-8 hours to complete, depending on how good you are at puzzles in general. Has very little mechanical requirements after you figured out a solution for a puzzle (akin to Portal). Overall more than worth its money.
P. S. Also this is finally a game, that provides translation to Olzard language out of the box, Hopefully, this would start a new trend and other games would soon follow its example.

205913774.review: insanely good game. highly recomend. pixel graphics are great. no puzzle is too difficult.

205903773.review: So far I love it. Fun knowledge based unlocks / MetroidBrainia puzzles. Will write more when I'm finished.

205883825.review:
This does everything right. A really rewarding gameplay loop that seems to have some excellent flow! Saw Olexa playing a few moments of it, decided it was my jam, and turned off the video (Sorry, Tyler!). It's been just an hour and change so far, but the brain is hitting the dopamine puzzle hits of Animal Well.

This (at least at this point!) is quite a phenomenal little game. I'll update after I finish the game.

Update: That was a fun time. Get it, finish it, and then go back and get some of the stuff you missed. It's intuitive, fun, and I'm gonna miss it. Play it.

205883774.review: Overall the puzzles are fun and it's an interesting game overall. The idea that the number of rooms you've been in being important and limited lead to some interesting puzzles. Like most time loop games needing to move through the same areas and do some of the same things again is slightly tedious but it's an otherwise tightly enough designed game where it wasn't too bad.

205879375.review: Charming little 'Metroidbrainia' style game. Took me about 4.5 hours to fully clear the game + secret ending without hints. The puzzles are pretty clever, with a couple stumping me for a bit. If you're in the mood for a brain teasing little adventure game to play over an afternoon or weekend, there's certainly worse ways you could spend $10. :D

205879252.review: Great MetroidBrainia to fill the hole left after completing Outer Wilds!

205876655.review: Game is extremely well designed. It teaches you as you go without holding your hand. Hard enough that it makes you go AHA!!! but not so hard you need to google anything

205861652.review: The puzzles were great. I loved how the limited amount of moves allowed for some unconventional problem solving. I won't spoil much, but just the duh moment I had when trying to figure out how to listen to the mother singing was great. Really enjoyable overall. Only thing I could request is an easier way of actually restarting in the graveyard area without having to exit the scene, and the ability to skip the intro cutscene for other reasons (I might be exceedingly clumsy). Other than that, I genuinely enjoyed it.

205861149.review: Absolutely fantastic game. I love the art style, the puzzles were great, and it was a compelling story.

205857734.review:
Really nice timeloop puzzles! Everything is very streamlined, though sometimes it feels like you have to explore until you find somewhere where the game gives you the answer. Good few hours of brain-scratching and time loop fun.

Ending spoilers:
[spoiler]The idea of an inevitable time loop where the beginning causes the end is very intriguing to me, especially if you factor in the idea of a perfected true ending...[/spoiler]

205825715.review: Quick 2-3 hour game, honestly worth the price given the gameplay in my opinion. Puzzles are great, tho some puzzles are simple but others really require you to think [spoiler]especially the lost woods and the glasses[/spoiler]. There are also times where I've needed to actually read and think about the text like [spoiler] when the lizard said his cart that picks up rocks didn't hit me that the lizard was implying the rock next to him [/spoiler]. Otherwise great game, actually makes you think a bit and plan ahead of time.

205770952.review: This is a very nicely executed, pixel-styled metroidbrainia. The primary mechanic is reminiscent of the steps limit in Blue Prince, but there's no RNG involved here at all (a blessing for me). There is also no timer or other contrived mechanism to impede your progress - you only have to be creative in how you use your limited steps. Also the in-game hint system was mostly helpful to me in the places I needed it.

205750793.review: Just play it. You are going to love it.

205740911.review:
I very much like timeloop games, and Chronoquartz is no exception. This bite-sized package is jam-packed full of creative puzzles and well worth its price. 5 hours is the average time, I think, but if you're really good, you could probably get a full run done in around 5-10 minutes. ;)
I did get stuck for a little bit of time in a couple places, but usually thinking about the situation in different ways helped.

205730163.review:
Short fun puzzle game about time travel that took 6 hours including true ending but used the ingame hint system for the true ending. The puzzled arent too difficult but a few of them took 15-20mins of thinking for me to get through. Highly recommend the experience if your into puzzle games. 

There is no timing puzzles its all based on how many screens you move.

205729071.review:
I'll start with the cons before describing the pros: 

Puzzle game fans that prefer their games to make them stop and think for any stretch of time, this isn't going to give you that. Most of the gameplay takes the form of doing the rather obvious next thing, where you're then told another rather obvious next thing to do. I do say *most* because there are a handful of puzzles near the end of the game that might take you a minute.

The graphics remind me of a certain kind of mobile game; that's largely a personal gripe that doesn't mean much. The game obviously doesn't have the best looks in the world, but it didn't try to; there's certainly nothing wrong with the graphics either, and they do the job just fine.

(Really, I'm probably being much too harsh here because of bad games I've played that look similar, but this game isn't like that.)

The pros:
This game is remarkably respectful of your time. One of the major design difficulties of time loop games is the larger than average amount of backtracking and otherwise frustrating game design inherent to the concept; Chronoquartz does a spectacular job of not being very frustrating to play at all, actually. 

Most information you need to remember is placed on the map screen where you can review it whenever you need to, meaning you never need to backtrack to a piece of information that you need for a puzzle. The exception to this is anything written in a text box; but it's very clear when there's something in a text box that you might need to take note of.

Additionally, I mentioned the difficulty isn't very high in this game; that's largely because everything interacts almost exactly like you would *expect* it to - which is a good thing! Puzzle games like this can often have the classic point and click problem of needing to put X thing in Y place when it makes no sense. In this game, it just always makes sense, and it's small enough that you never really have time to forget about stuff that might be useful from earlier.

And just because they aren't difficult doesn't mean they aren't satisfying. Every new area of the game introduces new mechanics that keep things fresh, and reaching the ending of each area usually involves tying everything together in a way that feels very nice to do. The secret/true of the game also take a bit of thought to figure out, and is a satisfying experience as well.

Also, moving around the objects isn't annoying. I feel like it really easily *could've* been annoying, I feel like it's the sort of mechanic that could easily cause a lot of frustration if it isn't handled well, but in this game it causes none, and I feel like that's worth praising.

Overall, for ten bucks, you'll get a satisfying ~5 hour journey that'll make you think just enough that you'll be content with your time.

205698781.review: A good albeit short little puzzler (my playtime includes playing through it again for missed achievements).  The time loop mechanic is squeezed out in variety of clever ways, and there is always something new around the corner to distract you from the fact that the puzzles themselves never get too challenging

A suggestions for new players is that most items have one use so don't be too afraid to leave them after you get to use them (what I mean is don't hoard cuz you can't)

Learning the world around you in this game is super important when solving puzzles. Skipping just one dialog and not experimenting can be a huge pain in the butt when you don't know what to do - love it.
I really hope for a sequel or at least an expansion.
